sections.title: A Special Day in School
sections.content_summary: The section opens with a Republic Day celebration at school, where students gather at the flagpole to watch the tricolour flag unfurl and sing the National Anthem together. Students learn the significance of each colour on the National Flag — saffron for strength and courage, white for peace and truth (with the Ashoka Chakra representing dharma), and green for growth and prosperity. The section also explains why Republic Day is celebrated on 26 January 1950, the day India adopted its Constitution, linking freedom from British rule (15 August 1947) to the need for a governing set of rules.

tasks.task_text: What special events or activities take place at your school on the occasion of Republic Day?
We sing the National Anthem together on this day! Can you name some rivers and mountain ranges mentioned in the National Anthem? Fill in the table given below.
On Republic Day, which activities would you like to participate in?
Have you ever watched the Republic Day parade in Delhi on television? What did you like the most about it?
Discuss and list down the various items of your state tableau. Make a poster or a model of it.
What events take place at your school on the Independence Day?
Write down five rules that are followed at your school. If you were to make one new rule, what would it be?

sections.title: Finding India in Currency Notes
sections.content_summary: Students are invited to observe an Indian currency note closely, discovering layers of meaning embedded in its design — Mahatma Gandhi's portrait (including a hidden watermark image), the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan spectacles symbol (inspired by Gandhi ji's cleanliness values), the National Emblem (three lions representing strength, courage, and confidence, with the Ashoka Chakra), and features like raised prints and the MANI app that help visually impaired citizens identify notes. The section uses the currency note as a window into India's history, civic values, and national symbols. Students fill in an observation table recording denominations, colours, languages, monuments, animals, and other symbols on the note.

tasks.task_text: Take any Indian currency note and observe it carefully. Then, fill in the table below.
Design an emblem for your school. Think of the message you want to convey by using symbols, words, shapes and colours to make it special.

sections.title: Symbols that Speak
sections.content_summary: This section introduces India's national symbols — the National Emblem (Ashoka's lion capital), the National Animal (tiger), and the National Bird (peacock) — as expressions of national identity and pride. Students encounter riddles to identify the tiger and peacock, and learn that lions, once widespread across India, now survive only in the Gir forests of Gujarat thanks to conservation efforts. The section also prompts students to discover their own state's symbols (animal, bird, tree, flower) and to design a school symbol by spotting a local natural element.

tasks.task_text: Read the riddles given below and guess the national symbols of India:
If we had to choose a national fruit for our country, which one would you suggest? Tell us why.

States also have some symbols. With the help of your teacher, find out the following symbols of your state.
State animal : _____________  State bird : _____________
State tree : _____________  State flower : _____________
Spot a tree, bird, animal, flower or object near your school. Choose one as your school symbol. Draw it and write one line on why it can be your school's symbol.

sections.title: Our Vibrant Culture
sections.content_summary: India's cultural richness is explored through the lens of linguistic diversity — more than a thousand languages are spoken across the country, each carrying its own history, songs, and stories. Students discover the word for 'mango' in Tamil (Māmpalam) and are encouraged to find its name in Telugu, Marathi, Bengali, and other languages spoken by classmates. The section also introduces regional diversity in food, clothing, songs, and dances, framing this variety as a source of national strength, and notes that digital translation apps now make cross-language learning accessible.

tasks.task_text: Find out about the languages spoken by students in your class and name them.
Pick a specific word (for example, mango) and have fun learning what it is called in different languages.

sections.title: Diversity Everywhere
sections.content_summary: This section uses the metaphor of a forest versus a garden to explain how diversity strengthens a nation — just as a diverse forest is more resilient and self-sustaining than a monocultural garden, India's diversity makes it stronger. Traditional headgear from various regions (saafa/pagri from Rajasthan, topi from Himachal Pradesh) is used to show how clothing signals regional identity, tradition, and cultural heritage. The section also covers functional headgear such as helmets for road safety and occupational protection, and features India's rich tradition of percussion instruments — dhol, tabla, mridangam, dholak, thavil, idakka, khol, nagara, pakhawaj, dhama, khanjira — each shaped by local culture and materials. Classical and folk dance forms from different states are also introduced.

tasks.task_text: In small groups, take a dupatta or scarf and help each other make a traditional headgear.
Create a headgear or cap using waste materials.
Under your teacher's guidance, identify and mark the states on the map of India where these dance forms originated.
What are some traditional Indian musical instruments that you know of?
Try to make a musical instrument from locally available materials.

sections.title: Spirit of Togetherness
sections.content_summary: The concluding section presents India as a shining example of unity in diversity — diverse languages, religions, clothing, and traditions coexist within a shared national identity, like threads woven into one vibrant fabric. India's heritage includes ayurveda, yoga, and meditation alongside modern achievements in space science, digital payments (UPI, BHIM), and fast rail networks. Students are invited to reflect on currency note monuments, currency symbols, national contributions of great Indians, and to conduct a 'Cultural Fair' in class where groups research and present different states of India.

tasks.task_text: If you could learn one more Indian language, which one would you select and why?
Which monuments do you see in different currency notes? Make a table.
Identify the currency symbol of India among the images shown below (the clue is in our currency note). Which other country's currency symbol can you identify?
Which animals do you see in a ₹10 note?

You must have seen pictures of a few great people of India in your school or any other place. Identify the names of these great men and women shown below, and write a few lines about their contributions.
Find out from your elders about people, who have contributed to the nation from your locality or state, and share what you learnt in the classroom.

Project: Cultural fair — Celebrate the vibrant diversity of India, where every state tells its own colourful story. Divide the class into different groups. Assign each group a different state of India. Each group will explore their assigned state and prepare a presentation covering—traditional dress, popular food, festivals, languages, dance or music. Set up a cultural fair in the class and discuss unique features and common values found across states.

dual_strand.natural: India's geographic and ecological diversity — rivers, mountain ranges, forests, and wildlife habitats that shape the country's natural landscape
dual_strand.human_cultural: India's socio-cultural unity in diversity — national symbols, constitutional values, languages, headgear, music, dance, and the spirit of togetherness (Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam)
